pairs.question: What was the original purpose of HMS Lofoten when it was built in 1945?
pairs.answer: HMS Lofoten was originally built as one of a large number of Landing Ship Tanks (LSTs) in 1945, designed for amphibious operations.

pairs.question: How was HMS Lofoten involved in the Suez Crisis?
pairs.answer: During the Suez Crisis, HMS Lofoten was used to transport and land the forces of 45 Commando as part of the first-ever operational helicopter assault.

pairs.question: What modifications were made to HMS Lofoten in 1964, and why?
pairs.answer: In 1964, HMS Lofoten was converted into a dedicated helicopter training ship. The deck in front of the superstructure was converted into a landing platform to facilitate helicopter landings and takeoffs at sea, though it lacked a hangar and substantial support facilities.

pairs.question: What role did HMS Lofoten serve after being replaced as a helicopter training ship in 1967?
pairs.answer: After being replaced by RFA Engadine in 1967, HMS Lofoten was laid up in 1969 and repurposed as an accommodation ship and support vessel. From at least 1981 onwards, it was docked alongside nuclear submarines undergoing refit at Rosyth Royal Dockyard.

pairs.question: Which other ship served alongside HMS Lofoten in supporting nuclear submarine refits at Rosyth?
pairs.answer: HMS Stalker, another LST, was also used as a support vessel for nuclear submarine refits at Rosyth, operating in the second of the two nuclear-qualified docks.

pairs.question: What happened to HMS Lofoten in 1993?
pairs.answer: In 1993, HMS Lofoten was sold for scrapping after serving in various roles including accommodation and support vessel.

pairs.question: Why was HMS Lofoten named after the Norwegian islands raided during Operation Claymore in 1941?
pairs.answer: HMS Lofoten was named after the Norwegian islands targeted during Operation Claymore in 1941 as part of a historical naming tradition, reflecting the ship's eventual role in amphibious and helicopter operations.
